title: Loops and range iteration

canonicalExplanation.summary: C++ loops maintain invariants and progress, while range-for expresses traversal through begin and end and inherits iterator lifetime and invalidation rules.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A const auto& range-for reads each large string without copying and does not mutate the vector during traversal. Your goal is to implement and debug loops over numeric ranges and standard-library sequences. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Program structure and main; pf state expressions; if and switch. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: C++ loops maintain invariants and progress, while range-for expresses traversal through begin and end and inherits iterator lifetime and invalidation rules. This rule is applied to concrete C++ source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Mutating a container can invalidate iterators, references in range-for control copying, unsigned countdown can wrap, and views can dangle. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: auto in a range-for always refers to the original element, so edits through it update the container.

includes: Choose counted, condition-controlled and range-based iteration while preserving termination and bounds.
Implement and debug loops over numeric ranges and standard-library sequences.
excludes: Mutating a container can invalidate iterators, references in range-for control copying, unsigned countdown can wrap, and views can dangle.

outcomes: Implement and debug loops over numeric ranges and standard-library sequences.

examples.title: Loops and range iteration: defined case
examples.situation: A const auto& range-for reads each large string without copying and does not mutate the vector during traversal.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: A const auto& range-for reads each large string without copying and does not mutate the vector during traversal.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: auto copies each element, while auto& binds to the element; choose reference and const qualification according to intended mutation and cost.
examples.result: The defined result follows because auto copies each element, while auto& binds to the element; choose reference and const qualification according to intended mutation and cost.

examples.title: Loops and range iteration: boundary case
examples.situation: An index loop uses i < values.size() and performs zero iterations for an empty vector.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: An index loop uses i < values.size() and performs zero iterations for an empty vector.
Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: Mutating a container can invalidate iterators, references in range-for control copying, unsigned countdown can wrap, and views can dangle.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: auto in a range-for always refers to the original element, so edits through it update the container.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Implement and debug loops over numeric ranges and standard-library sequences.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: auto in a range-for always refers to the original element, so edits through it update the container.
misconceptions.correction: auto copies each element, while auto& binds to the element; choose reference and const qualification according to intended mutation and cost.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete CPP rules, explain why this belief fails: auto in a range-for always refers to the original element, so edits through it update the container.

analogy.example: A system of self-managing components can model Loops and range iteration: each component protects an invariant and owns resources through typed interfaces and lifetime.
usefulFor: Connecting types, value semantics, invariants, generic interfaces, ownership, and deterministic cleanup
limitations: C++ overload resolution, templates, value categories, object lifetime, and undefined behavior have no exact physical equivalent.
